title: Uncovering Exotic Paired States in the 2D Spin-Imbalanced Fermi Gas with Neural Wave Functions
authors: Wan Tong Lou, Gino Cassella, Andres Perez Fadon, Halvard Sutterud, David Pfau, James S. Spencer, Johannes Knolle, W. M. C. Foulkes
abstract: We study the zero-temperature phase diagram of the 2D spin-imbalanced Fermi gas with short-ranged attractive interactions using the recently developed neural network variational Monte Carlo method with the AGPs FermiNet Ansatz. The Fulde-Ferrell-Larkin-Ovchinnikov phase is observed in the weakly interacting BCS limit and a polarised superfluid is seen in the strongly interacting BEC limit. When the interactions are strong, the minority-spin momentum density is reduced almost to zero in the momentum-space region occupied by the unpaired majority-spin electrons. When the interactions are very strong, phase separation occurs, with regions containing bosonic pairs and unpaired regions occupied by the remaining majority-spin particles. In addition, we observe translational symmetry breaking at intermediate interaction strengths, where the system forms an exotic crystal of Cooper pairs in a Fermi fluid of unpaired majority-spin particles. We provide a possible explanation for the formation of the crystalline phase, explain the origins of the k-space momentum-density hole when the pairs are tightly bound, and discuss how our approach opens new directions for future work.
summary: This paper explores the phase diagram of a 2D spin-imbalanced Fermi gas using neural network-based quantum Monte Carlo. It reveals the existence of an exotic crystalline phase of Cooper pairs at intermediate interaction strengths, situated between the FFLO phase and a phase-separated BEC limit. The work demonstrates how neural wave functions can capture complex correlations and spontaneous symmetry breaking in strongly interacting many-body systems.
main_problem: Investigating the zero-temperature phase diagram and exotic paired states of a 2D spin-imbalanced Fermi gas across the BCS-BEC crossover.
main_result: The study identifies three distinct regimes: an FFLO phase in the weak-interaction BCS limit, a new crystalline phase of Cooper pairs in the intermediate regime, and a phase-separated Bose-Fermi mixture in the strong-interaction BEC limit.
method: Neural Network Variational Monte Carlo using the AGPs FermiNet Ansatz, optimized via JAX-based KFAC gradient descent.
model_or_system: A 2D spin-imbalanced Fermi gas with short-ranged attractive interactions, modeled using a modified Pöschl-Teller potential in a periodic square box.
key_observables: Momentum density (spin-resolved), condensate fraction, one-particle real-space density, two-body density matrix, and pairing energy.
important_parameters: Interaction strength (v0), scattering length parameter (eta), and spin imbalance (N_up/N_down ratio).
main_assumptions: The study is subject to finite-size effects due to the small number of particles simulated, though the phase diagram is argued to be qualitatively robust.
figures_summary: Figures show momentum density evolution, condensate fraction across the crossover, and real-space density maps illustrating the formation of a crystalline lattice and phase separation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem and model, describes the Neural Network VMC methodology, presents the results across three interaction regimes (BCS, crossover, BEC), and discusses the physical origins of the observed phases.
why_interesting: It uses advanced machine learning architectures (FermiNet) to discover a previously unpredicted crystalline phase in a purely attractive system, driven by emergent effective repulsion.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Mean-field (BCS) momentum density of the SIFG. Left: regime (I), weakly interacting SIFG. The crosses and circles indicate the occupied spin-up and spin-down momentum states of the (N ↑, N ↓) = (25, 9) SIFG. The wavy purple line shows the optimal pairing between opposite spins in the weakly interacting limit. The spin-up and spin-down Fermi circles of the infinite system are colored pink and blue-gray, respectively. Right: regime (III), strongly interacting SIFG. The mauve region indicates the diffuse momentum density arising from the tightly-bound real-space pairs. The area of the Fermi circle with radius ˜kF matches the density of unpaired majority-spin fermions.

FIG. 2: Momentum density of the (N ↑, N ↓) = (25, 9) SIFG for a range of different interaction strengths across the BCS-BEC crossover. The top and bottom rows show the momentum densities of spin-up and spin-down particles, respectively. The strength of the interaction, v0, increases from left to right. To aid comparison, all momentum densities are normalized such that their maximum value is equal to one. This is accomplished by multiplying the momentum density by the number in the top-left corner in each subfigure.

FIG. 3: Condensate fraction of the (N ↑, N ↓) = (25, 9) SIFG for a range of different interaction strengths across the BCS-BEC crossover. The condensate fraction in each subfigure has been rescaled to aid comparison, with the maximum condensate fraction shown in the top-left corner of each subfigure.

FIG. 4: One-particle density of the (N ↑, N ↓) = (25, 9) SIFG for a range of different interaction strengths across the BCS-BEC crossover. The plots on the top row are the densities of spin-up particles and the plots on the bottom row are the densities of spin-down particles.

FIG. 5: Occupied momentum states of the SIFG in our simulations. The red crosses and the blue circles represent spin-up (majority) and spin-down (minority) particles, respectively. The big red and blue dashed circles represent the “Fermi surfaces”. The yellow curly lines denote one of the optimal finite-momentum pairing in each case.
FIG. 6: The spin-resolved momentum density and the band structure of the SIFG from the mean-field BCS calculations.
FIG. 7: Momentum density of the (N ↑, N ↓) = (13, 5) SIFG for v0 = 0.1, 0.15, 0.2.
FIG. 8: Momentum density of the (N ↑, N ↓) = (13, 5) SIFG for v0 = 0.25, 0.3, 0.35.
FIG. 9: Momentum density of the (N ↑, N ↓) = (13, 5) SIFG for v0 = 0.4, 0.45, 0.5.
FIG. 10: Momentum density of the (N ↑, N ↓) = (25, 9) SIFG for v0 = 0.1, 0.15, 0.2.
